coredata.dc:title: Dual-responsive carbon dots-based luminophore for ratiometric fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence detection of oxytetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qin
Tan, Qiuxia
Zhao, Shaojing
Zhang, Kui
Chen, Jianmin
Lan, Minhuan
coredata.dc:description: Dual-responsive probes emitting both fluorescence and phosphorescence signals are greatly advantageous to sensing. Here, we present a carbon dots-based luminophore (CDs@LP) with blue fluorescence and ultra-long yellow room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) with lifetime of 1.33 s (that was visible to the naked eye for ∼ 13 s) for ratiometric detection of oxytetracycline (OTC). The CDs@LP was prepared by molten salt method through one-step treatment of 2, 6-diaminopyridine, KNO3, MgCl2, and KH2PO4. Upon the addition of OTC into CDs@LP aqueous solution, the fluorescence at 386 nm and RTP at 538 nm of CDs@LP were quenched as a result of the inner filter effect (IEF), while the fluorescence at 522 nm of OTC steadily enhanced due to the molecular restriction through the formation of OTC-Mg2+ complexes. Both fluorescence and RTP signals have excellent linear relationships with OTC concentration. The CDs@LP had a low detection limit of 3.2 nM (fluorescence) and 0.27 μM (phosphorescence), which also exhibited satisfactory selectivity and anti-interference performance towards other antibiotics. Moreover, the CDs@LP was successfully loaded on a filter paper and used for OTC sensing. Thus, it is a promising tool for OTC detection and can act as a foundation for producing dual-signal sensing tools.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-responsive
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Oxytetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection mechanism of CDs@LP toward OTC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration demonstrating the principle of CDs@LP in dual-signal detection of OTC.
(a) Synthesis process, (b) TEM image, (c) FTIR spectrum, (d) XPS spectrum, and (e) XRD spectrum of CDs@LP.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@LP (0.15 mg/mL) and (b) phosphorescence spectra of CDs@LP (1.25 mg/mL) aqueous solution under different excitation wavelengths. (c) Time-dependent photographs of CDs@LP powder before and after turn-off the 365-nm light irradiation.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@LP aqueous solution upon addition of OTC from 0 to 20 μM. (b) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio I522/I386 and OTC concentration. The inset shows the fluorescence color of the CDs@LP aqueous solution without OTC and with OTC (20 μM) upon irradiation with 365-nm UV light. (c) Fluorescence spectra of inorganic salt shell aqueous solution upon addition of OTC from 0 to 20 μM. (d) Fluorescence spectra of 10 μM OTC in the presence of metal ions. (e) Absorption spectra of inorganic salt shell aqueous solution upon addition of OTC from 0 to 20 μM. (f) Absorption spectrum of OTC and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs@LP.

(a) Effect of pH on the I522/I386 of CDs@LP in the absence and presence of 10 μM OTC. Time-dependent I522/I386 of CDs@LP in different (b) concentration of OTC and (c) pH of the solution. (d) Selectivity and anti-interference performance of CDs@LP toward 10 μM OTC in the presence of other antibiotics.

(a) Phosphorescence spectra of CDs@LP aqueous solution in response to OTC at concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μM. (b) Linear relationship between phosphorescence intensity and OTC concentration. Photographs of CDs@LP in (c) aqueous solution and (d) test strips. Red dashed lines represented the control group without OTC, while blue dashed lines represented CDs@LP with OTC. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Ratiometric fluorescence and RTP detection of OTC in actual samples.

head.ce:title: Dual-responsive carbon dots-based luminophore for ratiometric fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence detection of oxytetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Putian University, Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Analysis and Laboratory Medicine (Putian University), Fujian Province University, Putian, Fujian 351100, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Analysis and Laboratory Medicine (Putian University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Putian University, Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Analysis and Laboratory Medicine (Putian University), Fujian Province University, Putian, Fujian 351100, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual-responsive probes emitting both fluorescence and phosphorescence signals are greatly advantageous to sensing. Here, we present a carbon dots-based luminophore (CDs@LP) with blue fluorescence and ultra-long yellow room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) with lifetime of 1.33 s (that was visible to the naked eye for ∼ 13 s) for ratiometric detection of oxytetracycline (OTC). The CDs@LP was prepared by molten salt method through one-step treatment of 2, 6-diaminopyridine, KNO3, MgCl2, and KH2PO4. Upon the addition of OTC into CDs@LP aqueous solution, the fluorescence at 386 nm and RTP at 538 nm of CDs@LP were quenched as a result of the inner filter effect (IEF), while the fluorescence at 522 nm of OTC steadily enhanced due to the molecular restriction through the formation of OTC-Mg2+ complexes. Both fluorescence and RTP signals have excellent linear relationships with OTC concentration. The CDs@LP had a low detection limit of 3.2 nM (fluorescence) and 0.27 μM (phosphorescence), which also exhibited satisfactory selectivity and anti-interference performance towards other antibiotics. Moreover, the CDs@LP was successfully loaded on a filter paper and used for OTC sensing. Thus, it is a promising tool for OTC detection and can act as a foundation for producing dual-signal sensing tools.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probes are widely used in the analysis and detection of important biological molecules and environmental pollutants due to their many advantages including simple equipment, convenient operation, high sensitivity, and dynamic real-time monitoring [1–3]. However, only relying on the change of fluorescence signal easily leads to false positive results, especially in sensing samples which can generate auto-fluorescence [4] . The development of probes with multi-parameters or multi-responsiveness can effectively improve the selectivity and accuracy of the detection. Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have gained widespread attention and have been extensively applied in the fields of bio-imaging, optoelectronic devices, anti-counterfeiting and biochemical sensing owing to their long-lived naked-eye-recognizable luminescence, large Stokes shift, good stability and high signal to noise ratio [5–10]. Some organic RTP materials have been developed as probes for detecting small organic molecules, heavy metal ions, and some environmental factors such as oxygen, humidity, and pH [11–14]. However, the preparation process of these organic materials is complex, and the conditions required for producing RTP are harsh.

Carbon dots (CDs), as novel photoluminescent materials, have the advantages of simple synthesis process, easy surface functionalization, adjustable emission spectrum, good water solubility, and high biocompatibility [15–18]. Moreover, the unique micro-nano structure and surface chemistry of CDs provide a new idea for the development of novel RTP materials [19–25]. Fluorescence and RTP CDs-based probes can bring new opportunities to the accurate and rapid visual detection of targets [26–29]. For example, Lu et al. synthesized P-CDs that emit fluorescence at 418 nm and RTP at 525 nm. The addition of tetracyclines (TCs) can quench both the fluorescence and RTP of the CDs due to the inner filler effect (IEF) [30]. Su et al. prepared N-CDs which emit both blue fluorescence and green RTP. The blue fluorescence of N-CDs is quenched by promethazine (PMZ) at a concentration range of 0.4 ∼ 8 mM through the electron transfer mechanism. Additionally, the phosphorescence of N-CDs was found to steadily enhance as the PMZ concentration was increased, and the lifetime of RTP also increased from 429.88 to 565.19 ms [31].

However, the principles of the above probes are based on the intensity variation of fluorescence and RTP which can be influenced by the pH, probe concentration, temperature, and other environmental conditions [32–34]. In contrast, ratiometric signals could provide more accurate information for the sensing process [35,36]. Here, we present a CDs-based luminophore (CDs@LP) with blue fluorescence and yellow RTP with ultra-long lifetime of 1.33 s, that was visible to the naked eye for ∼ 13 s, for ratiometric detection of oxytetracycline (OTC). As shown in Fig. 1 , in the presence of OTC, both the fluorescence and RTP of CDs@LP aqueous solution were quenched due to the IEF within 50 s. However, the green fluorescence of OTC at 522 nm was generated and enhanced due to the molecular restriction by Mg2 + doped in CDs@LP [37]. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I522/I386) and the RTP intensity ratio ((I0-I)/I0) had excellent linear relationship with OTC concentration. CDs@LP also had a low limit of detection (LOD) of 3.2 nM (fluorescence) and 0.27 μM (phosphorescence), as well as good selectivity and anti-interference performance, thus is a promising tool for dual-signal detection of OTC. These outcomes could also be distinctly observed in CDs@LP containing filter paper.

KNO3, MgCl2, and KH2PO4 were purchased from Titan Co. Ltd. OTC, TC, doxycycline hydrochloride (DC), chlortetracycline hydrochloride (ClTC), chloramphenicol (ClMC), roxithromycin (RMX), amoxicillin (AMC), ampicillin (APC), gentamicin sulfate (GTMC), kanamycin sulfate (KNMC), and streptomycin sulfate (SMC) were obtained from Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. Metronidazole (MTZ), ornidazole (ONZ) and tinidazole (TNZ) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. Carbenicillin disodium (CCD) was obtained from Xiya Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. 2, 6-Diaminopyridine was purchased from Heowns Co., Ltd.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed on a Talos F200X G2 transmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a Nicolet iS20 IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a VG ESCALAB 220i-XL surface analysis system. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was collected on a Rigaku Miniflex 600. Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were determined using RF-6000 and UV-2600 spectrophotometers, respectively. Fluorescence lifetimes were determined on an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer. The phosphorescent spectra were obtained on F-7000 spectrophotometer under 340 nm excitation. The excitation and emission slits were 20 nm, and the PMT voltage was 1000 V. The phosphorescent lifetimes at 538 nm were collected on FluoroMax + spectrofluorometer under 340 nm excitation.

KNO3 (2 g), MgCl2 (0.2 g), KH2PO4 (0.1 g), and 2, 6-diaminopyridine (0.05 g) were added into 10 mL of ultrapure water. The solution was then freeze-dried to obtain solid powder. After being ground evenly, the powder was transferred into a crucible, which was then placed in a muffle furnace at 400 ℃ to allow the components in the powder to react for 3 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained caky solid was ground to produce CDs@LP powder. CDs@LP (0.5 g) was dispersed in 20 mL ultrapure water and treated with ultrasound for 1 h to obtain CDs@LP aqueous solution.

The CDs@LP aqueous solution (0.625 mg/mL) was prepared by mixing 50 μL of the above solution with 1950 μL of HEPES buffer solution (20 mM, pH = 7.7). For OTC detection, OTC at different concentrations (0–20 μM) was mixed into the CDs@LP aqueous solution. The fluorescence spectra of the mixture were then collected at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

OTC at concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μM was mixed with 1.25 mg/mL CDs@LP aqueous solution. Then, the phosphorescence spectra of the mixture were collected at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. A filter paper was used to fabricate a paper OTC detection platform. The CDs@LP aqueous solution was dropped onto a filter paper. After drying naturally, the CDs@LP based-test strip was obtained. In OTC detection, OTC solution was dropped onto the prepared fluorescence and RTP test strip. The test strips were then irradiated under 365 nm UV light and photographs of the test strips were taken using a mobile phone, which with and without OTC were compared.

2, 6-Diaminopyridine was used as a sole carbon source, KNO3, KH2PO4, and MgCl2 were used as a mixed inorganic salt matrix to prepare CDs@LP using a molten salt method (Fig. 2 a) [38]. At 400 °C, the inorganic salts melted into liquid, while CDs were formed in situ. After cooling down to room temperature, the molten inorganic salts recrystallized to serve as a rigid shell that can confine and protect CDs. TEM image (Fig. 2b) confirmed that CDs were confined by inorganic salt matrix; they were monodispersed and had a uniform size with an average diameter of 2.6 nm (Fig. S1). They had obvious lattice spacing of 0.20 nm (inset in Fig. 2b), which corresponds to the (100) plane of fthe crystal structure of CDs [39].

The surface groups and chemical compositions of CDs@LP were characterized by FTIR and XPS. As revealed in Fig. 2c, the FTIR spectrum of the CDs@LP exhibits the characteristic band belonging to –NH/–OH at ∼ 3440 cm − 1. The peaks at 2918 cm − 1 and 1077 cm − 1 can be assigned to PO43−/HPO42−/H2PO4 − groups [40] The peak at 1382 cm − 1 is attributed to C-O/C-N/NO3 −. The peaks at 1760 cm − 1 (C = O), 1632 cm − 1 and 822 cm − 1 (CC) were also observed. Furthermore, the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2d) demonstrated that CDs@LP was composed of C, N, O, Mg, P, Cl, and K with contents of 57.5%, 7.61%, 21.68%, 0.94%, 0.67%, 2.26%, and 9.39%, respectively. Moreover, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Mg 1s, P 2p, and Cl 2p shown in Fig. S2 suggested the presence of C–O, CO, C–N, C–P, CC, PO groups in CDs@LP, which are consisted with the FTIR spectrum. XRD pattern indicated the presence of amorphous and crystalline phases in CDs@LP. As shown in Fig. 2e, the broad peak at 24.7° can be assigned to CDs, which is indicative of highly disordered carbon atoms [41]. Besides, inorganic salt shell contained three magnesium salts, MgHPO4·3H2O, Mg3(PO4)2, and MgO, as indicated by the peaks at 23.7°, 29.6°, and 41.3°, respectively [42]. These data further demonstrate that recrystallized inorganic salts act as a rigid matrix to protect and confine CDs in situ.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs@LP aqueous solution showed a distinct peak at 295 nm (Fig. S3a), which could be attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C. There was also a wide absorption band at a range of 300 nm to 600 nm, which could be assigned to the n-π* transition of C = O/C = N. The fluorescence spectra of CDs@LP exhibited a red-shift as the excitation wavelength was increased from 320 nm to 380 nm (Fig. 3 a). On the contrary, the RTP spectra did not display obvious excitation wavelength-dependent behavior (Fig. 3b). This may be the generation of CDs@LP was from the carbon core at high temperature, while that of the RTP was due to the one phosphorescent center of surface state [43,44]. Besides, the RTP excitation spectra of the CDs@LP overlapped with the absorption spectra at a range of 300 nm to 500 nm (Fig. S3b), demonstrating the phosphorescence originates primarily from C = O/C = N of CDs@LP [45]. As expected, the CDs@LP powder emitted blue fluorescence upon being excited with light at a wavelength of 365-nm, and emitted bright yellow phosphorescence after the 365-nm light was turned off, and this could be observed with the naked eye for ∼ 13 s. The photoluminescence quantum yield of CDs@LP was measured to be 5.5%. As shown in Fig. S4, the phosphorescent lifetime of CDs@LP in solid could be fitted with a double-exponential function with two lifetime components of 467.12 ms (29.82%) and 1702.64 ms (70.18%), and thus the average lifetime was calculated to be 1.33 s. While the phosphorescence lifetime of CDs@LP in solution could be also fitted with two lifetime components of 510.42 ms (36.85%) and 1795.92 ms (63.15%), and the average lifetime was calculated to be 1.32 s, which were longer than most RTP materials.

The CDs@LP was used as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for OTC detection. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity at 386 nm of CDs@LP aqueous solution gradually decreased as the OTC concentration was increased from 0 to 20 μM. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity at 522 nm of OTC significantly enhanced to ∼ 100 k, which was more than ten times than the fluorescence intensity of 20 μM OTC in the absence of CDs@LP (Fig. S5). As can be observed inset of Fig. 4b, the fluorescence color of the CDs@LP aqueous solution was changed from blue to green after the addition of OTC. The fluorescence intensity ratio, I522/I386, had a satisfactory linear relationship with the OTC concentration (R2 = 0.997). Based on three parallel titration experiments, the LOD of OTC was calculated to be 3.2 nM, which was lower than most of probes (Table S1). Besides, as shown in Fig. S3a, the absorption spectra showed that the absorbance at 370 nm increased with the increase of OTC concentration, and the two parameters also had a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.999; Fig. S6b). The above results demonstrate that the CDs@LP is a potential ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of CDs@LP toward OTC
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the detection mechanism of CDs@LP toward OTC, we prepared the inorganic salt shell using the same condition (but without 2,6-diaminopyridine). The aqueous solution of the inorganic salt shell did not exhibit fluorescence (Fig. 4c). By contrast, strong green fluorescence peaked at 522 nm was observed with increasing OTC concentration from 0 to 20 μM. To further find the factors causing the enhancement of fluorescence, several metal ions at the same concentration were added to OTC aqueous solutions. As exhibited in Fig. 4d, only Mg2 + significantly caused the fluorescence enhancement. In addition, the absorption spectra of the inorganic salt shell in the presence of OTC at different concentrations (Fig. 4e) showed a peak at 370 nm, which is in agreement with the result shown in Fig. S6a. However, the maximum absorption wavelength of OTC was measured to be 360 nm, as depicted in Fig. S7, suggesting the interaction between OTC. The above results suggested that the increase of fluorescence at 522 nm is due to the coordination between Mg2 + in inorganic salt shell and OTC, and the coordination can restrict the non-radiative vibration of OTC.

Factors causing the decrease of fluorescent intensity at 386 nm of CDs were also explored. As shown in Fig. 4f, the absorption spectrum of OTC and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs@LP were largely overlapped, which suggests that OTC quenched the fluorescence of CDs@LP via the IEF mechanism. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs@LP solution in Fig. S8a with three components of 0.37 ns, 2.45 ns, and 10.00 ns was calculated to be 2.22 ns, and this changed to only 2.14 ns (which is negligible) after OTC was added (Fig. S8b), further confirming the IEF quenching mechanism.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence of CDs@LP aqueous solution in the absence and presence of OTC was investigated. Due to the protective effect of rigid inorganic salt matrix, the fluorescence spectra of CDs@LP were unchanged under different pH values (Fig. S9a). In contrast, the fluorescence intensity at 522 nm increased due to the improved fluorescence intensity of OTC with the increase of pH from 6.8 to 8.2 (Fig. S9b and S10). The fluorescence intensity ratio I522/I386 also increased with increasing pH and then gradually stabilized at pH 7.7 (Fig. 5 a). Next, we studied the responsive time between the CDs@LP and OTC. According to Fig. S11 and Fig. 5b, OTC could quickly quench the fluorescence of CDs@LP within 50 s. Besides, the CDs@LP could react with OTC quickly under different pH values (Fig. 5c & Fig. S12). These results manifest that CDs@LP aqueous solution can rapidly detect OTC under mild conditions.

Subsequently, the selectivity and anti-interference performance of the CDs@LP aqueous solution were evaluated in the presence of different kinds of antibiotics. As shown in Fig. S13a, CDs@LP aqueous solution only responded to OTC. We have investigated the fluorescence spectra response of CDs@LP to the other three tetracycline antibiotics including TC, DC, and ClTC. As shown in Fig. S14, similar fluorescence spectral changes were observed upon addition of TC, DC, and ClTC, i.e., the fluorescence intensity at 386 nm was decreased, while the fluorescence intensity at 522 nm was increased as increasing the concentration of tetracycline antibiotics. However, the LOD of CDs@LP toward TC, DC, and ClTC is 2.9, 5.1, 9.8 nM, respectively, which may be due to the different coordination abilities of CDs@LP with tetracycline antibiotics. The fluorescence intensity ratio I522/I386 of the CDs@LP aqueous solution in the presence of other antibiotics was similar to that of the control group (Fig. 5d). Despite the interference by other antibiotics, CDs@LP aqueous solution could remain highly responsive to OTC (Fig. S13b).

Due to the presence of rigid and insoluble inorganic salt matrix, the phosphorescence of CDs@LP could not be quenched by water and dissolved oxygen. As shown in Fig. S15, the phosphorescence intensity of CDs@LP aqueous solution has no difference under N2 atmosphere and air atmosphere. Thus, the CDs@LP could also be regarded as a phosphorescence probe for the detection of OTC. As exhibited in Fig. 6 a, the phosphorescence could be quenched by OTC at concentrations from 0 to 100 μM, due to the IEF. Additionally, the phosphorescence intensity had a good linear relationship with OTC concentration from 0 to 60 μM (Fig. 6b). The LOD of phosphorescent signal was calculated to be 0.27 μM after five parallel experiments, which were more sensitive for analyte than most of other two-signal probes in Table S2. The bright yellow phosphorescence of CDs@LP in the absence of OTC could be obviously observed for ∼ 6 s; on the other hand, the phosphorescence intensity of CDs@LP was significantly decreased after the addition of OTC (Fig. 6c).

We also investigated the OTC-sensing ability of fluorescence and RTP test strips, as shown in Fig. 6d. Upon irradiation with 365-nm UV light, the test strips in the absence of OTC exhibited blue fluorescence, while those in the presence of OTC exhibited green fluorescence. After the UV light was turned off, the test strips both in the absence and presence of OTC exhibited yellow phosphorescence. However, the phosphorescence was brighter and lasted longer in the absence of OTC. Besides, we added different concentrations of OTC onto the test strips. As exhibited in Fig. S16a, with the increase of OTC concentration, the fluorescence color of the test strips transferred from blue to green, and the phosphorescence intensity decreased gradually. The three-primary colors G/B signal of test strips obtained from a smartphone application has a good linear relationship with the concentration of OTC, suggesting this CDs@LP test strip has the ability to semi-quantitatively detect OTC (Fig. S16b). In addition, we also studied the selectivity and anti-interference performance to other antibiotics by using phosphorescence signal. As shown in Fig. S17, the phosphorescence of CDs@LP exhibited good selectivity and anti-interference for OTC. These observations show that CDs@LP can serve as an optical probe for ratiometric fluorescence and phosphorescence two-signals detection of OTC.

In order to testify the practical application, we also tested the recovery rate in actual water samples. As shown in Table 1 , the fluorescence signals of CDs@LP to different concentrations of OTC (6, 10, 14 μM) in river water and tap water were detected with good recovery rate in the range of 99.82% to 104.02% (Fig. S18a & b). And the phosphorescence signals of CDs@LP towards OTC (10, 30, 50 μM) were measured to be ranged from 99.50% to 113.39% (Fig. S18c & d). These data certified the feasibility of CDs@LP for ratiometric fluorescence and RTP detection of oxytetracycline sensitively in practical application.

In conclusion, the present work demonstrated that CDs@LP exhibited blue fluorescence and long-lived phosphorescence (lifetime of 1.33 s) in aqueous solution; thus, it could be regarded as a dual-responsive probe that can selectively and accurately detect OTC. CDs@LP aqueous solution had a low LOD of 3.2 nM (fluorescence) and 0.27 μM (phosphorescence) for OTC. The ratiometric fluorescence detection of the probe was achieved via the IEF and covalent restriction effect between Mg2 + of inorganic salt shell and OTC. The phosphorescence detection was possible due to the quenching effect caused by the IEF. In addition, CDs@LP was dropped onto a filter paper to generate fluorescence and RTP test strip, which can have the potential to apply in many fields, including the optical detection. This work offers new ideas for the design and application of two-signal fluorescence and phosphorescence probes for the sensing field.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62175262), the Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (No. 2022RC1201), Key R & D plan of Hunan Province (No. 2022SK2101), the Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Analysis and Laboratory Medicine (Putian University), Fujian Province University (No. PALM 202201, PALM202202).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.144061.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A two-pronged detection of atherosclerosis with a dual-channel fluorescent probe for viscosity and hypochlorous acid
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. K. Wang, S. Wang, B. Y. Wang, J. L. Shen, L. L. Zhao, F. B. Yu, J. T. Hou, A Two-pronged Detection of Atherosclerosis with a Dual-channel Fluorescent Probe for Viscosity and Hypochlorous Acid, Chem. Eng. J. 464 (2023) 142687, 10.1016/j.cej.2023.142687.

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Color-tunable ultralong organic room temperature phosphorescence from a multicomponent copolymer
